book_summary: Deuteronomy has chapters 1-20 complete in restored/source-audited draft and mystical companion layers. Moses' opening discourse and central law now cover historical memory, Shema and covenant love, centralized worship, dietary holiness, tithes and local food security, debt release, materially supported servant liberation, pilgrimage festivals, impartial courts, limited kingship, accountable prophecy, refuge cities, witness protections, warfare exemptions, siege ethics, and fruit-tree preservation. Difficult laws involving iconoclasm, apostasy executions, destroyed cities, religious courts, forced labor, captive-taking, and total war are preserved under explicit anti-persecution, anti-authoritarian, anti-slavery, anti-genocide, and non-reenactment safeguards.

chapters.notes: Centralized worship, household rejoicing, local meat slaughter, blood as life, Levite inclusion, child-sacrifice rejection, and iconoclasm under non-reenactment safeguards.
Signs do not authenticate a false path; prophet, intimate-enticer, and apostate-city execution laws preserved under categorical anti-persecution safeguards.
Mourning boundaries, dietary distinctions, pilgrimage tithe, practical monetary conversion, Levite inclusion, and third-year food security for immigrant, orphan, and widow.
Seventh-year debt release, open-hand lending, poverty responsibility, Hebrew servant release with productive assets, permanent-service tension, and firstborn animals.
Passover, Weeks, Booths, inclusive festival joy, proportionate gifts, local judges, anti-bribery law, justice pursuit, and cult-object restrictions.
Sacrificial integrity, idolatry adjudication, multiple witnesses, central court, and king limits on horses, wives, wealth, and pride through daily Torah.
Levitical support and mobility, child-sacrifice and divination prohibitions, prophet like Moses, failed-word test, and false-prophet death law under non-enforcement safeguards.
Reachable refuge cities, intent analysis, blood-avenger restraint, boundary stones, two-or-three-witness requirement, false-witness investigation, and proportional retaliation.
Battle address, civilian-life and fear exemptions, coercive peace and forced labor, captive-taking, total-destruction commands, and protection of fruit trees during siege.

translation_notes: Deuteronomy remains Moses' retrospective covenant interpretation and must be compared with earlier Torah narratives.
The Shema, festivals, dietary practice, tefillin, mezuzah, and covenant election remain living Jewish inheritance and must not be superseded or appropriated against Judaism.
Centralized-worship and anti-image laws must never authorize destruction of another community's sacred sites, art, or memory.
A sign or fulfilled prediction is not self-authenticating; claims remain accountable to covenant context, truth, and ethical testing.
Apostasy, false-prophet, court-contempt, and idolatry death penalties are source-preserved religious-persecution laws and categorically barred from modern enforcement.
Dietary holiness is not disgust, and sacrificial blemish language never defines the worth of disabled people.
The third-year tithe, debt release, open-hand lending, and servant manumission with productive assets make economic justice material rather than symbolic.
Freedom without resources is abandonment; Exodus memory requires a released servant to leave with flock, grain, and wine.
Festival joy is incomplete without servants, Levites, immigrants, orphans, and widows.
Justice must resist partiality and bribery; difficult cases need appeal, but religious courts must not become unaccountable authoritarian power.
The king remains a brother under Torah and is forbidden the classic accumulations of empire: war horses, wives, and excess wealth.
Failed prophecy should release people from fear; it may not be rescued through manipulation or shifting claims.
Refuge requires roads and geographic access; due process requires intent analysis, multiple witnesses, and investigation of accusers.
Lex talionis limits escalation and must not mandate mutilation, execution, or revenge.
Deuteronomy 20 contains genuine restraints inside an unacceptable war system; exemptions and fruit-tree protection do not sanitize forced labor, captive-taking, or genocide.
Mystical companion layers remain interpretive and never replace restored/source-audited text.
source_witness_audit_flags: Deuteronomy 12 centralization and chosen-place language require comparison with Leviticus, Numbers, Shiloh/Jerusalem traditions, and Jewish reception.
Deuteronomy 12 cult-site destruction requires anti-iconoclasm and anti-colonial safeguards.
Deuteronomy 13 fulfilled signs, intimate denunciation, and apostate-city herem require maximum religious-freedom and anti-persecution metadata.
Deuteronomy 14 animal and bird identifications require lexical/zoological audit, and tithe structures require comparison with Numbers and later Jewish practice.
Deuteronomy 15 release terminology, foreigner distinction, servant status, and furnishing obligations require economic and slavery-history audit.
Deuteronomy 16 festival centralization, inclusive joy, judicial appointments, and justice formula require liturgical and legal-reception audit.
Deuteronomy 17 high-court authority and monarchy restrictions require later biblical monarchy and rabbinic legal comparison.
Deuteronomy 18 prohibited-practice vocabulary, prophet-like-Moses interpretation, and failed-prophecy test require Jewish reception and anti-persecution safeguards.
Deuteronomy 19 refuge geography, blood-avenger institution, boundary law, witness procedure, and talion require legal-history audit.
Deuteronomy 20 war exemptions, forced labor, distant-city captives, local herem, and fruit-tree law require maximum anti-war, anti-slavery, anti-sexual-violence, and ecological safeguards.
next_actions: Continue in a large batch with Deuteronomy 21-28: unsolved murder, captive woman, inheritance and rebellious-son laws, corpse dignity, neighbor and property duties, sexual and assembly laws, divorce and worker protections, levirate marriage, weights, Amalek, firstfruits and third-year tithe declarations, covenant ceremony, and the full blessing-and-curse chapter—with trauma-informed, anti-misogyny, anti-slavery, anti-capital-punishment, and anti-abuse safeguards.
